title: Estenosis Espinal Lumbar
subtitle: Bloque C — Neurocirugía Espinal: Estenosis Degenerativa, Claudicación Neurógena, Descompresión Quirúrgica
guidelines_version: NASS Clinical Guidelines 2020 · AOSpine Degenerative Lumbar Stenosis Guidelines 2020 · NICE NG59 2016
textbook_refs: Benzel EC. Spine Surgery: Techniques, Complicación Avoidance, and Manejo (3rd ed, 2012)
Weinstein JN et al. Surgical versus Nonsurgical Therapy for Estenosis Espinal Lumbar. NEJM 2008;358:794-810 (SPORT)
Malmivaara A et al. Surgical or Nonoperative Treatment for Estenosis Espinal Lumbar? Spine 2007;32:1-8
Forsth P et al. A Randomized, Controlled Ensayo of Fusion Surgery for Estenosis Espinal Lumbar. NEJM 2016;374:1413-1423 (SWESPINE)
learning_objectives: Clasificar lumbar spinal stenosis anatomically (central, lateral recess, foraminal) and correlate with clinical presentation
Distinguir neurogenic claudication from vascular claudication using history, examination, and imaging features
Aplicar NASS evidence-based conservative management algorithm before recommending surgery
Seleccionar the appropriate decompressive surgical procedure (laminectomy, bilateral decompression via unilateral approach, TLIF/PLIF) based on stenosis pattern and presence of instability
Interpretar SPORT spinal stenosis cohort data including crossover bias and as-treated versus intention-to-treat analyses
Identificar radiological grading systems (Schizas, Verbiest) and clinical outcome measures (ZCQ, VAS, ODI) used in stenosis research

blocks.content:
**Lumbar spinal stenosis (LSS)** se define como narrowing of the spinal canal, lateral recesses, or intervertebral foramina sufficient to cause compression of neural elements and associated clinical symptoms. It es el más común reason for spinal surgery in patients over 65 years and represents a major public health burden in ageing populations. The aetiology is overwhelmingly degenerative in adults, though congenital narrowing (short pedicles, trefoil canal morphology in achondroplasia) can predispose individuals to symptomatic stenosis at younger ages and with less degeneration.

The degenerative cascade begins with disc desiccation and loss of disc height, which transfers increased load to the posterior elements — particularly the facet joints. Facet joint arthrosis leads to osteophyte formation, synovial cyst development, and hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum (LF). While disc bulging contributes anteriorly, LF hypertrophy is the dominant posterior compressive element, often reaching 5-8 mm in thickness (normal <4 mm). In spondylolisthesis, anterior translation of one vertebral body over another further narrows the canal dynamically, with the narrowing worsening in extension and partially relieved in flexion — the anatomical basis for the characteristic posture-dependent symptoms.

Anatomically, LSS is classified as **central canal stenosis** (compression of the thecal sac), **lateral recess stenosis** (compression of the nerve root before it exits the neural foramen, typically by a hypertrophied superior articular process or posterior disc), and **foraminal stenosis** (compression of the exiting nerve root within or beyond the foramen, often by facet osteophyte or foraminal disc herniation). These patterns frequently coexist. The anteroposterior (AP) diameter of the lumbar spinal canal is normally 15-25 mm; absolute stenosis se define como AP diameter <10 mm, and relative stenosis as <12 mm, though cross-sectional area (<75 mm² for relative, <100 mm² for borderline) and the Schizas grading (A=visible CSF, B=no visible CSF anterior to cord, C=nerve roots not visible, D=signal change) are increasingly preferred in contemporary literature.

blocks.title: Estenosis en Tándem — No Pasar por Alto
blocks.content: In patients with bilateral leg symptoms and signs of spinal cord dysfunction (hyperreflexia, Babinski, hand clumsiness), always image the entire spine. Tandem cervical-lumbar stenosis is present in 5-25% of surgical candidates with lumbar stenosis. Decompressing the lumbar spine in isolation when significant cervical myelopathy exists can unmask or worsen myelopathy — cervical decompression should generally be performed first.

The hallmark clinical syndrome of central lumbar spinal stenosis is **neurogenic claudication (NC)**, also termed pseudoclaudication. Patients describe bilateral (or unilateral) leg pain, heaviness, numbness, or paraesthesiae that develop with walking or prolonged standing and are relieved by sitting, squatting, or forward lumbar flexion. The relief with flexion occurs because lumbar flexion increases the cross-sectional area of the spinal canal by unfolding the ligamentum flavum, increasing the anteroposterior diameter by up to 40%. The classic 'shopping cart sign' captures this: patients lean forward on the shopping trolley handle to flex their lumbar spine, allowing longer distances to be walked without symptoms.

The pathophysiology of NC involves a combination of direct mechanical compression of nerve roots and ischaemic mechanisms. During exercise, metabolic demand of the cauda equina nerve roots increases, but compressed, epidurally restricted roots cannot receive adequate blood flow. This vascular steal phenomenon explains why symptoms may occur with standing alone (not just walking) and why recovery requires sitting rather than simply stopping movement — a key distinguishing feature from true vascular claudication, where stopping walking (and thus reducing lower limb blood demand) provides rapid relief within seconds to minutes regardless of posture.

Radiculopathy from lateral recess or foraminal stenosis presents differently: unilateral dermatomal leg pain in a specific distribution, often with sensory loss and reflex changes, similar to disc herniation but with a typically more insidious onset. The 'two-level' or 'multi-level' nature of degenerative stenosis means radicular patterns may be complex or bilateral. Physical examination in pure central stenosis is often unremarkable between episodes — the Romberg test and bicycle test can be informative. Neurological deficits (weakness, reflex loss) when present in isolation suggest root compression at lateral recess or foraminal level.

blocks.title: Escenario Clínico — Prueba de Alivio con Flexión
blocks.content: Un hombre de 72 años walks 100 metres before bilateral leg pain forces him to stop. He leans on his walking frame and the pain resolves in 3 minutes. He then rides a stationary bicycle for 20 minutes without symptoms. ABI is 1.1 bilaterally. This combination — posture-dependent claudication, relief with flexion, cycling tolerance, and normal ABI — es patognomónico de neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis. Vascular investigations are confirmatory but not required to proceed to spinal imaging.

**MRI of the lumbar spine** is the investigation of choice for lumbar spinal stenosis. It provides soft tissue detail of the thecal sac, ligamentum flavum, disc, facet joints, and nerve roots without radiation. The key imaging features are: LF hypertrophy (>4 mm on axial T2), facet joint arthrosis with osteophyte formation, loss of the normal epidural fat signal surrounding the thecal sac on axial T2 images, nerve root clumping, and reduced or absent CSF space around the cauda equina. Spondylolisthesis is graded using the Meyerding classification (I=<25% slip, II=25-50%, III=50-75%, IV=>75%) and is identified on lateral standing radiographs, which are essential as MRI performed supine may underestimate dynamic instability.

The **Schizas grading** (A-D on axial T2) is the most widely used qualitative grading system: Grado A demonstrates visible CSF anterior and posterior to the nerve roots; Grado B has no CSF visible anterior to the rootlets (thecal sac indented but nerve roots still visible); Grado C has no CSF and rootlets not visible as separate entities; Grado D has no rootlets visible and the canal has a homogeneous signal. Grados C and D correspond to severe stenosis requiring surgical consideration when clinical symptoms align.

For surgical planning, **CT myelography** offers superior bony detail and is preferred when MRI está contraindicado or when prior posterior instrumentation creates artefact. Dynamic (flexion-extension) radiographs assess instability — translation >4 mm or angulation >10-15° between segments indicates instability requiring fusion in addition to decompression. **Standing full-spine radiographs (EOS system)** are increasingly used to assess coronal and sagittal balance when deformity surgery is contemplated, with particular attention to lumbar lordosis (normal L1-S1: 40-80°, ideal ~20° greater than pelvic incidence), pelvic incidence-lumbar lordosis mismatch, and sagittal vertical axis.

blocks.title: Perla de Examen — Correlación Imagen-Síntoma
blocks.content: Radiological severity does NOT always correlate with clinical severity. Up to 21% of asymptomatic adults over 60 have MRI evidence of moderate-to-severe lumbar stenosis (Boden et al., JBJS 1990). Surgery is indicated by the clinical syndrome of neurogenic claudication, not by MRI grade alone. Treat the patient, not the scan.

The natural history of lumbar spinal stenosis is not uniformly progressive. Several natural history studies, including the Johnsson cohort (1992, 32 patients followed 4 years: 70% stable, 15% improved, 15% deteriorated) and the SPORT non-operative arm (2008), demonstrate that many patients remain stable or improve without surgery over 4-year follow-up, although the majority have persistent symptoms and functional limitation. This supports a trial of structured conservative management as first-line treatment for most patients without progressive neurological deficits.

**Conservative management** encompasses several evidence-based modalities. Physiotherapy focusing on lumbar flexion-based exercises and core stabilisation provides modest benefit; the Norwegian group (Mieminen) demonstrated short-term improvement in walking capacity. Structured exercise programmes outperform passive modalities. **Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)** provide modest analgesic benefit but debe usarse cautiously in elderly patients given GI, renal, and cardiovascular risks. **Gabapentinoids** (gabapentin, pregabalin) target neuropathic pain components and may improve claudication distance, though evidence is limited. **Epidural corticosteroid injections (ESIs)** — particularly transforaminal or caudal approaches — can provide short-term relief (6-12 weeks) and may defer surgery, but do not alter long-term outcomes (MILD-II trial, Friedly et al. NEJM 2014: ESI + gabapentin vs ESI alone — no significant difference at 6 months). The MILD (Minimally Invasive Lumbar Decompression) procedure, using a percutaneous fluoroscopic approach to remove hypertrophied ligamentum flavum tissue via a posterior interlaminar window, has shown benefit in appropriately selected patients with predominant LF hypertrophy.

Patient education, weight reduction in obese patients, and assistive walking devices (rollator frame promotes forward flexion) all contribute to functional improvement. The NASS guidelines (2020) recommend structured conservative management for 4-6 weeks before surgical referral in patients without progressive neurological deficit or cauda equina compromise.

blocks.title: Esteroides Epidurales — Evidencia SPORT y MILD-II
blocks.content: The MILD-II trial (Friedly et al. NEJM 2014) randomised 400 patients with LSS to ESI alone vs ESI plus the MILD procedure, finding no significant difference at 6 months (primary outcome PROMIS-PF). Epidural corticosteroid injections provide 3 months of modest benefit (NNT ~6 for >30% pain reduction) but do not prevent eventual surgery — approximately 57% of patients receiving ESI proceed to surgery within 2 years.

blocks.title: Síndrome de Cauda Equina — Emergencia Quirúrgica Absoluta
blocks.content: The onset of bilateral progressive leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia (S2-4 dermatomes), and urinary retention or incontinence in a patient with known LSS indicates acute cauda equina compression and requires emergency MRI and urgent surgical decompression within 24 hours. This presentation overrides any conservative pathway — proceed directly to surgery after confirming the diagnosis. See C2 chapter for detailed CES protocol.

sections.title: Ensayo SPORT — Base de Evidencia para Tratamiento Quirúrgico vs Conservador

blocks.content:
The **SPORT (Spine Patient Resultados Research Ensayo)** represented the largest randomised controlled trial of surgical versus non-surgical treatment for lumbar spinal stenosis, with results published in NEJM in 2008 by Weinstein et al. The trial enrolled 289 randomised patients and 365 observational patients with symptomatic LSS at 13 US academic and community spine centres, with 4-year follow-up.

The randomised cohort suffered a critical methodological limitation: **40% crossover** from the non-surgical to surgical arm within the first 3 months, rendering the intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis statistically non-significant for all primary outcomes at 3 months and 2 years. Sin embargo, both the ITT and **as-treated analyses** showed progressive convergence of outcomes at 4 years, with the surgical group maintaining a clinically meaningful but non-statistically-significant advantage in the ITT analysis.

In the **as-treated analysis**, surgical patients showed significantly greater improvement at all time points through 4 years compared with non-surgical patients in: SF-36 bodily pain (primary outcome), SF-36 physical function, Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), and patient satisfaction. At 4 years, surgical patients had 24-point greater improvement in SF-36 bodily pain (P<0.001). The **observational cohort** yielded similar results without crossover contamination, with surgical benefit evident at 3 months and sustained through 4 years.

Key lessons from SPORT for clinical practice: (1) surgery provides faster and more complete relief than conservative care, particularly in the first 2 years; (2) with conservative management, roughly 50% of patients improve meaningfully over 4 years; (3) delayed surgery (after 3-12 months of conservative treatment) does not worsen outcomes, justifying the sequential approach recommended in guidelines. The **SWESPINE trial (Försth et al., NEJM 2016)** addressed the fusion question: decompression alone versus decompression plus fusion for LSS with or without spondylolisthesis — with no significant difference in primary outcomes at 2 years (ODI, patient satisfaction), challenging the routine addition of fusion for all stenosis-with-spondylolisthesis cases.

headers: Mejora en Dolor Corporal SF-36 (Cirugía vs No-cirugía)

blocks.title: Perla de Examen — SPORT ITT vs Por Protocolo
blocks.content: Exam questions about SPORT frequently test the distinction between ITT and as-treated analyses. The ITT analysis was NON-SIGNIFICANT because of 40% crossover from non-surgical to surgical arms. The as-treated analysis showed highly significant benefit for surgery at all time points through 4 years. The correct conclusion: SPORT does not prove equivalence — it demonstrates that crossover contamination precluded meaningful ITT analysis, while as-treated data support surgical superiority for symptom relief.

sections.title: Técnicas Quirúrgicas: Indicaciones de Descompresión y Fusión

blocks.content:
The surgical goal in lumbar spinal stenosis is adequate neural decompression while preserving spinal stability. The choice of procedure depends on stenosis pattern (central vs lateral vs foraminal), presence of degenerative spondylolisthesis or instability, number of levels involved, and patient-specific factors (osteoporosis, comorbidities, sagittal balance).

**Laminectomy** — complete removal of the spinous process, bilateral laminae, and flavum — provides excellent central and bilateral lateral decompression but sacrifices posterior osseoligamentous tension band integrity. Single-level laminectomy carries a low (5-10%) risk of post-laminectomy instability; multi-level laminectomy or laminectomy in the setting of degenerative spondylolisthesis significantly increases this risk. For this reason, **bilateral decompression via unilateral approach (BDUA)**, also called 'over-the-top' or 'contralateral undercutting laminotomy', has become the preferred technique at many centres: a unilateral keyhole laminotomy is performed, the microscope or endoscope is angled across the midline to decompress the contralateral lateral recess and foramen, preserving the supraspinous and interspinous ligaments, spinous processes, and contralateral musculature. This technique se asocia con equivalent decompression, less blood loss, shorter hospital stay, and reduced destabilisation compared with total laminectomy (Thome et al., J Neurosurg 2005).

**Fusion indications** remain debated following the SWESPINE trial. The consensus from NASS guidelines (2020) is that fusion should be added when: (1) degenerative spondylolisthesis >4 mm translation or progressive slippage is present; (2) dynamic instability is demonstrated on flexion-extension films (>4 mm translation); (3) scoliotic deformity with coronal imbalance; (4) iatrogenic instability (bilateral facetectomy >50% at multiple levels). Fusion techniques include: **TLIF (transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion)** — posterior approach, unilateral facetectomy, PEEK or titanium interbody cage with pedicle screw fixation; **PLIF (posterior lumbar interbody fusion)** — bilateral facetectomy, higher neural mobilisation risk; **ALIF (anterior lumbar interbody fusion)** — anterior approach for L4-5, L5-S1, superior disc height restoration. **Minimally invasive (MIS) TLIF** is increasingly used: tubular retractor system, smaller incision, less blood loss, faster recovery — equivalent fusion rates to open TLIF at 2 years.

For isolated lateral recess stenosis, a **medial facetectomy** (removing the medial third to half of the superior articular process) decompresses the lateral recess and traversing root without destabilisation. Foraminotomy addresses the foraminal exit zone. The concept of **'decompression without fusion for stable LSS'** and **'fusion added for instability'** should guide surgical planning rather than institutional preference.

blocks.title: Surgical Options for Estenosis Espinal Lumbar — Seleccionarion Guide
rows: Preserve posterior tension band; <50% facet preserved
Check far-lateral disc via Wiltse approach if needed

blocks.title: Contexto Clínico — Implicaciones del Ensayo SWESPINE
blocks.content: The SWESPINE trial (Försth et al., NEJM 2016) randomised 247 patients with LSS with or without spondylolisthesis to decompression alone versus decompression plus instrumented fusion (PLIF or posterolateral fusion). At 2 years, there was NO significant difference in primary outcome (ODI score: mean change -20 decompression vs -18 decompression+fusion, P=0.24) or walking ability. Fusion did not improve outcomes even in the spondylolisthesis subgroup. Sin embargo, the trial used fixed pedicle screw systems and did not assess patients with dynamic instability or progression — these high-risk groups were likely excluded. The practical implication: do NOT add fusion routinely for stable spondylolisthesis; reserve it for documented dynamic instability.

sections.title: Complicaciones Quirúrgicas, Resultados y Selección del Paciente

blocks.content:
Surgical decompression for LSS is generally safe with appropriate patient selection, but carries procedure-specific and patient-specific risks. **Dural tear** es el más común intraoperative complication, occurring in 2-9% of primary cases and up to 14% of revision cases. Manejo: primary reparación hermética with 4-0 or 5-0 Prolene ± injerto graso reinforcement, 24-48 hour prone positioning avoided in favour of flat supine bed rest to prevent pseudomeningocele. An unrepaired dural tear may result in fístula de LCR, wound breakdown, or meningitis.

**C5 palsy equivalent (nerve root palsy after decompression)** occurs in approximately 1-2% of patients, most commonly affecting L5 after wide L4-5 decompression. Mecanismo: tethered root injury or hyperaemia reperfusion. The majority resolve spontaneously within 6 months. **Post-operative spondylolisthesis (iatrogenic instability)** is reported in 5-15% following laminectomy without fusion, particularly at L4-5 with pre-existing disc degeneration. Patients require surveillance imaging at 6-12 months. **Epidural haematoma** following decompression presents with acute back/leg pain and neurological deterioration in the first 24-48 hours post-operatively and requires emergency return to theatre.

**Predictors of favourable surgical outcome** include: symptoms predominantly of claudication (walking limitation) rather than axial back pain; MRI severity (Schizas C-D) correlating with clinical symptoms; duration of symptoms <2 years; absence of severe comorbidities; and patient psychological profile. The **Zurich Claudication Questionnaire (ZCQ)** is the validated disease-specific outcome measure: symptom severity scale (1-5) and physical function scale (1-4) — change of ≥0.5 points is considered clinically significant. **Oswestry Disability Index (ODI)** is the standard generic outcome measure; minimal clinically important difference (MCID) is 12.8 points.

For elderly patients (≥75 years), LSS surgery yields equivalent improvement in claudication symptoms compared with younger patients (Jansson et al. 2006, Swedish Spine Register data), but with higher complication rates — particularly pulmonary, urinary, and delirium. Comorbidity assessment using ASA physical status, cardiac risk (RCRI), and frailty indices (CFS) should guide the decision to operate and the extent of surgery. Same-day surgery (ambulatory laminectomy) is feasible for well-selected patients and increasingly practised in high-volume centres.

Observation; steroids if early; majority resolve 6 months
