blocks.title: Perla de Examen — Fusión en Espondilolistesis tras SWESPINE
blocks.content: Before SWESPINE (2016), standard of care was decompression PLUS fusion for all LSS with spondylolisthesis. SWESPINE showed NO benefit of adding fusion at 2 years for stable spondylolisthesis. Current NASS 2020 guidelines: fusion is NOT routinely recommended for stable spondylolisthesis — reserve for dynamic instability (>4 mm translation on flex-ext radiographs) or progression during follow-up.

consolidation.intro: Pon a prueba tus conocimientos sobre lumbar spinal stenosis con 20 preguntas estilo examen que cubren fisiopatología, diagnóstico clínico, imagen, manejo conservador, indicaciones quirúrgicas y datos clave de ensayos.

questions.stem: Una mujer de 68 años complains of bilateral leg heaviness and paraesthesiae that begin after walking approximately 150 metres. She feels better when she leans forward on her shopping trolley. She can ride a stationary bicycle for 30 minutes without symptoms. On examination, peripheral pulses are present bilaterally and ankle-brachial index is 1.05 on both sides. El diagnóstico más probable es:

options.A: Enfermedad arterial periférica (estadio IIa de Fontaine)
options.B: Estenosis espinal lumbar con claudicación neurógena
options.C: Radiculopatía L5 bilateral por hernia discal
options.D: Neuropatía periférica diabética

explanation.correct: The combination of posture-dependent bilateral leg symptoms relieved by forward flexion (shopping cart sign), cycling tolerance (flexed lumbar spine), and normal ABI is pathognomonic of neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis. Vascular claudication is relieved by standing still (not by changing posture) and is worsened by cycling (increased metabolic demand). The normal ABI of 1.05 effectively excludes significant peripheral arterial disease as the primary cause.

wrong.A: Peripheral arterial disease with ABI >0.9 is not haemodynamically significant. Vascular claudication is relieved by standing still (not by sitting/leaning forward), and cycling increases calf blood demand — making it worse, not better.
wrong.C: Bilateral disc herniation causing bilateral L5 radiculopathy would produce dermatomal symptoms (dorsal foot, great toe) and would not be relieved by posture. The bilateral symmetrical walking-related pattern with posture relief is characteristic of central canal stenosis, not radiculopathy.
wrong.D: Diabetic neuropathy produces constant distal paraesthesiae worse at night ('restless legs'), not claudication-type symptoms that start with exercise and resolve with rest/flexion.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for Estenosis Espinal Lumbar (2020) — Recommendation for diagnosis: neurogenic claudication is the cardinal symptom, distinguished from vascular claudication by posture dependence and cycling tolerance.
explanation.trial: Johnsson KE et al. Natural course of lumbar spinal stenosis. Clin Orthop 1992;279:82-86: natural history cohort establishing the classic claudication syndrome as the diagnostic anchor for LSS.
explanation.pearl: The shopping cart sign (patients lean forward to flex lumbar spine and walk longer distances) is a functional real-world test of neurogenic claudication. ABI >0.9 excludes significant vascular claudication. These two features together confirm LSS without further investigation.

questions.stem: Un hombre de 73 años with lumbar spinal stenosis has axial T2 MRI showing loss of epidural fat anteriorly and posteriorly to the thecal sac, but individual nerve roots are still identifiable within the CSF. What is the Schizas grade, and what does it imply for management?

options.A: Grado A — no clinically significant stenosis; discharge with reassurance
options.B: Grado B — moderate stenosis; trial of conservative management appropriate
options.C: Grado C — severe stenosis; surgical decompression is mandatory
options.D: Grado D — very severe stenosis; emergency surgical decompression required

explanation.correct: Schizas Grade B is defined as: no visible CSF anterior to the thecal sac (loss of anterior epidural fat), but individual nerve rootlets are still identifiable within the dural sac. This represents moderate stenosis. Grade B is not an automatic surgical indication — a trial of structured conservative management (physiotherapy, NSAIDs, epidural injection) remains appropriate for 4-6 weeks unless the patient has progressive neurological deficits or cauda equina compromise.

wrong.A: Grado A describes visible CSF both anterior and posterior to the nerve roots, representing no significant stenosis. The description in this question (loss of anterior CSF, roots still visible) is Grado B, not Grado A.
wrong.C: Grado C se define como no CSF visible AND nerve roots not identifiable as separate entities. This question specifies that roots ARE still visible — placing it at Grado B, not C. While Grado C may warrant surgical consideration, it is still not 'mandatory' without correlating clinical severity.
wrong.D: Grado D features homogeneous signal throughout the canal with no rootlets visible. This question does not describe Grado D. Emergency surgery is reserved for acute cauda equina syndrome, not a radiological grade alone.

explanation.guideline: Schizas C et al. Qualitative grading of severity of lumbar spinal stenosis based on the morphology of the dural sac on MRI. Spine 2010;35:1919-1924 — the source publication for the Schizas grading system cited in NASS 2020 guidelines.
explanation.trial: SPORT Spinal Stenosis Cohort (Weinstein et al. NEJM 2008): non-surgical patients with moderate stenosis improved in approximately 50% of cases over 4 years, supporting conservative management as a legitimate initial strategy for non-severe, non-emergency presentations.
explanation.pearl: Schizas Grado: A = CSF visible anteriorly and posteriorly (no stenosis); B = no anterior CSF but roots visible (moderate); C = no CSF and roots not visible (severe); D = homogeneous signal, no structures visible (very severe). Only C and D are typically considered for surgery when symptoms correlate.

questions.stem: ¿Cuál de los siguientes is the PRIMARY anatomical contributor to lumbar spinal stenosis on the POSTERIOR aspect of the spinal canal?

options.A: Osificación del ligamento longitudinal posterior (OLLP)
options.B: Hipertrofia del ligamento amarillo
options.C: Complejo osteofítico discal posterior
options.D: Lipomatosis epidural

explanation.correct: Hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum (LF) is the dominant posterior contributor to lumbar spinal stenosis. As disc height decreases, the LF buckles inwards and hypertrophies, often reaching 5-8 mm (normal <4 mm), encroaching on the posterior spinal canal and lateral recesses. The LF is the primary target of surgical decompression (flavectomy). OPLL is a predominantly cervical pathology. Posterior disc osteophytes contribute anterolaterally, and epidural lipomatosis is a separate entity typically associated with corticosteroid use.

wrong.A: OPLL (Ossification of the Posterior Longitudinal Ligament) is classically a cervical spine disorder and affects the ANTERIOR spinal canal. It can occur in the lumbar spine but is rare and is not the primary cause of LSS.
wrong.C: The posterior disc-osteophyte complex contributes to ANTERIOR and anterolateral canal narrowing, not the posterior canal. The posterior disc bulge contributes, but the LF is dominant posteriorly.
wrong.D: Lipomatosis epidural (excessive epidural fat accumulation, associated with obesity, corticosteroid use) can cause neural compression but is not the primary or most common posterior contributor to degenerative LSS.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — Pathoanatomy section: ligamentum flavum hypertrophy identified as the primary posterior compressive element in degenerative LSS.
explanation.trial: Barz T et al. Nerve root sedimentation sign: evaluation of a new radiological sign in lumbar spinal stenosis. Spine 2010;35:892-897 — the nerve root sedimentation sign is caused by LF hypertrophy posterior compression preventing normal dependent settling of roots.
explanation.pearl: In lumbar spinal stenosis: the ANTERIOR compressor is the disc-osteophyte complex; the POSTERIOR compressor is the hypertrophied ligamentum flavum (LF). Surgical decompression must address both — anteriorly during distraction (disc space restoration) and posteriorly via flavectomy and undercutting facetectomy.

questions.stem: Una mujer de 77 años presents with progressive bilateral leg claudication at 50 metres. MRI shows severe central stenosis at L3-4 and L4-5 (Schizas C bilaterally). She has no history of spondylolisthesis on plain radiographs. Flexion-extension lateral views show no dynamic instability. ¿Cuál es el más apropiado surgical procedure?

options.A: Laminectomía L3-4 y L4-5 con fusión instrumentada TLIF
options.B: Laminotomía bilateral / descompresión bilateral por abordaje unilateral (DBUAU) en L3-4 y L4-5
options.C: Inserción de dispositivo interespinoso percutáneo (p.ej., X-STOP)
options.D: Infiltración epidural transforaminal de esteroides en ambos niveles y diferir cirugía

explanation.correct: For central lumbar spinal stenosis without instability or spondylolisthesis, decompression alone using bilateral decompression via unilateral approach (BDUA) is the evidence-based procedure of choice. BDUA preserves the posterior tension band (supraspinous and interspinous ligaments, contralateral musculature), reducing post-operative instability risk compared with full laminectomy. SWESPINE (2016) showed no benefit of adding fusion for stable presentations, and NASS 2020 recommends decompression alone for this scenario.

wrong.A: Instrumented fusion (TLIF) adds operative time, blood loss, implant cost, and complication risk without benefit when there is no spondylolisthesis or dynamic instability. SWESPINE (NEJM 2016) found no improvement in ODI or walking capacity when fusion was added to decompression in stable LSS, including the spondylolisthesis subgroup.
wrong.C: Interspinous spacer devices (X-STOP, Coflex) are appropriate for Grado B (moderate) stenosis in carefully selected patients who cannot tolerate general anaesthesia. They are not appropriate for severe (Schizas C) bilateral stenosis at two levels — decompression is needed.
wrong.D: Epidural steroid injection is a reasonable conservative measure to trial first, but this patient has severe Schizas C bilateral stenosis with very limited walking (50 metres) — she has already met the threshold where surgery is indicated. Further delay with ESI alone no es apropiado as definitive management.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — Grado B recommendation: decompression alone es apropiado for LSS without instability; fusion not routinely indicated in absence of dynamic instability or spondylolisthesis with translation >4 mm.
explanation.trial: Försth P et al. (SWESPINE). A Randomized, Controlled Ensayo of Fusion Surgery for Estenosis Espinal Lumbar. NEJM 2016;374:1413-1423: no significant difference at 2 years between decompression alone vs decompression+fusion for LSS ± spondylolisthesis.
explanation.pearl: For stable LSS (no instability, no spondylolisthesis >4 mm): BDUA (bilateral decompression via unilateral approach) is preferred over total laminectomy — it preserves the posterior tension band and reduces iatrogenic instability while providing equivalent decompression. No fusion needed.

questions.stem: The SPORT spinal stenosis cohort (NEJM 2008, Weinstein) failed to demonstrate significant surgical superiority in its randomised intention-to-treat analysis. La razón principal de este fallo estadístico fue:

options.A: The surgical technique (laminectomy) was inadequate for the degree of stenosis
options.B: El cuarenta por ciento del grupo no quirúrgico aleatorizado se cruzó a cirugía en 3 meses
options.C: The primary outcome measure (SF-36 bodily pain) was insensitive to surgical benefit
options.D: The follow-up period of 4 years was too short to detect differences

explanation.correct: The fundamental flaw invalidating the ITT analysis of the SPORT randomised cohort was massive crossover: approximately 40% of patients assigned to non-surgical treatment underwent surgery within the first 3 months, contaminating the control arm. This 'intent-to-treat' analysis compared a mixed (40% operated) non-surgical group against the surgical group, dramatically diluting any between-group difference. The as-treated analysis — which analysed patients according to the treatment they actually received — showed highly significant and sustained surgical benefit at all time points through 4 years.

wrong.A: Laminectomy was the standard procedure used in SPORT and es apropiado for central LSS. The surgical technique was not identified as a failure mode. The primary issue was study design contamination by crossover.
wrong.C: SF-36 bodily pain IS a validated, sensitive primary outcome for surgical spine studies and was selected a priori. The as-treated analysis using the same SF-36 BP scale DID show highly significant differences (P<0.001 at all time points), confirming the measure's sensitivity.
wrong.D: Four years is an adequate follow-up period for spine surgery trials. The non-significance was not a power/time issue — it was a crossover contamination issue. The observational cohort (which did not randomise) showed significant surgical benefit throughout the same time frame.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — SPORT referenced as Level I evidence for surgical benefit in as-treated analysis, with specific acknowledgement of ITT limitations due to crossover.
explanation.trial: Weinstein JN et al. Surgical versus Nonsurgical Therapy for Estenosis Espinal Lumbar. NEJM 2008;358:794-810 — ITT P=0.19 at 3 months; as-treated P<0.001 at 3 months and sustained through 4 years; 40% crossover documented.
explanation.pearl: SPORT ITT = non-significant (40% crossover contaminates). SPORT as-treated = highly significant surgical benefit at all time points through 4 years. This distinction es el más comúnly tested fact from SPORT in spine examinations.

questions.stem: Un hombre de 65 años with lumbar spinal stenosis undergoes L4-5 bilateral decompression via unilateral approach. He is awake and alert after surgery. At 8 hours postoperatively, he develops sudden severe lower back pain and bilateral leg weakness (Medical Research Council grade 3/5) with inability to void. He was pain-free and fully mobile 2 hours earlier. ¿Cuál es el siguiente paso inmediato?

options.A: RM de columna lumbar para descartar estenosis recurrente
options.B: Retorno urgente al quirófano para evacuación quirúrgica
options.C: Dexametasona IV 8 mg en bolo y reevaluar en 4 horas
options.D: Cateterización urológica y observación neurológica durante 24 horas

explanation.correct: The clinical picture — sudden onset severe back pain with rapid bilateral neurological deterioration (bilateral leg weakness + urinary retention) within 24 hours of lumbar decompression — is diagnostic of epidural haematoma until proven otherwise. This is a surgical emergency requiring IMMEDIATE return to theatre for haematoma evacuation. Delay beyond 6-8 hours markedly worsens neurological recovery. While MRI could confirm the diagnosis, it should NOT delay return to theatre if the clinical picture is unequivocal — theatre preparation should begin simultaneously with any imaging if theatre is not immediately available.

wrong.A: MRI would confirm an epidural haematoma, but in this acutely deteriorating patient, proceeding directly to theatre should NOT wait for MRI results. Clinical diagnosis is sufficient. If MRI does not delay theatre, it es apropiado — but answer B correctly prioritises surgical evacuation.
wrong.C: IV dexamethasone has no role in treating acute epidural haematoma. It would be appropriate for nerve root oedema or meningeal irritation, but the acute bilateral deterioration with retention is a compressive haematoma until proven otherwise — medical management alone is inappropriate.
wrong.D: Catheterisation addresses a symptom (retention) but does not treat the cause (haematoma compression). Neurological observation for 24 hours would be catastrophically wrong for an evolving epidural haematoma — the window for neurological recovery closes within hours.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — Post-operative complications: epidural haematoma presents with acute neurological deterioration and requires emergency surgical evacuation. Best outcomes when decompressed within 6-8 hours of symptom onset.
explanation.trial: Amiri AR et al. Postoperative spinal epidural haematoma: factors influencing surgical prognosis. Eur Spine J 2013;22:1387-1391: haematoma evacuation within 6 hours associated with complete neurological recovery; >12 hours associated with permanent deficits in the majority.
explanation.pearl: Acute neurological deterioration within 24-48 hours after lumbar decompression = epidural haematoma until proven otherwise = emergency return to theatre. Do NOT wait for MRI to delay surgical evacuation in clinically obvious cases.

questions.stem: Un paciente de 60 años undergoes lumbar laminectomy. During the procedure, the surgeon inadvertently tears the dura, resulting in a 5 mm rent with fístula de LCRage. The wound is still open. ¿Cuál es el correcto intraoperative management?

options.A: Proceed to closure immediately — the tear is small and will seal spontaneously
options.B: Primary repair with 4-0 or 5-0 non-absorbable suture ± injerto graso reinforcement
options.C: Place a drenaje lumbar preoperatively and close without direct repair
options.D: Flood the wound with cola de fibrina only, without suture repair

explanation.correct: Intraoperative dural tear management: the gold standard is primary watertight repair using fine non-absorbable suture (4-0 or 5-0 Prolene or nylon), reinforced with an autologous fat graft and/or dural sealant. The repair should be tested by Valsalva manoeuvre (surgeon asks anaesthestist to provide 30 cmH2O airway pressure) to confirm watertight closure. Fat graft from the subcutaneous layer provides an additional biological seal. Post-operatively, flat bed rest for 24-48 hours reduces hydrostatic pressure on the repair.

wrong.A: A 5 mm dural tear will NOT seal spontaneously in the immediate term and risks CSF cutaneous fistula, wound breakdown, meningitis, and pseudomeningocele formation. Leaving it without repair is surgical negligence.
wrong.C: Prophylactic drenaje lumbar es apropiado if primary repair is not achievable (e.g., calcified dura, wide tear), but it is NOT a substitute for primary repair when this is feasible. A drenaje lumbar reduces CSF pressure but does not close the dural defect.
wrong.D: Fibrin glue (e.g., Tisseel) is an adjunct to, not a replacement for, primary suture repair. Fibrin glue alone es insuficiente for a 5 mm tear under normal CSF pressure — it requires a sutured substrate to reinforce.

explanation.guideline: NASS Guidelines (2020) and Hannallah D et al. Manejo of líquido cefalorraquídeo leaks in spinal surgery. JAAOS 2008;16:456-466 — primary watertight suture repair is the standard of care for intraoperative dural tears when technically feasible.
explanation.trial: Desai A et al. Incidental durotomy during spine surgery: Systematic review of outcomes and complications. Eur Spine J 2015;24:287-295: primary repair with injerto graso reinforcement associated with lowest fístula de LCR rate (<5%) compared with cola de fibrina alone (>15% fistula rate).
explanation.pearl: Dural tear repair: (1) primary 4-0 Prolene suture — watertight; (2) injerto graso reinforcement; (3) Valsalva test intraoperatively; (4) flat bed rest 24-48h post-op; (5) sellante dural (DuraSeal, cola de fibrina) as adjunct only. Pseudomeningocele or persistent fistula = surgical re-exploration.

questions.stem: Un hombre de 71 años presents with bilateral leg pain and heaviness that limits his walking to 200 metres. MRI shows severe central stenosis at L4-5. He also has mild L4-5 degenerative spondylolisthesis with 3 mm slip on supine MRI. Flexion-extension lateral radiographs show no translation change between flexion and extension. According to SWESPINE trial data, the most appropriate management is:

options.A: L4-5 decompression plus TLIF instrumented fusion (standard for spondylolisthesis)
options.B: L4-5 decompression alone without fusion
options.C: Conservative physiotherapy — spondylolisthesis is a contraindication to surgery
options.D: Anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) at L4-5 to restore lordosis

explanation.correct: The SWESPINE trial (Försth et al., NEJM 2016) demonstrated that decompression plus fusion was NOT superior to decompression alone at 2 years for LSS with or without degenerative spondylolisthesis. This patient has stable spondylolisthesis (3 mm, no dynamic change on flex-ext views) — the key criterion for applying SWESPINE results. Decompression alone is appropriate, avoiding the increased operative time, blood loss, implant cost, and complication risk of instrumented fusion.

wrong.A: Before SWESPINE (2016), instrumented fusion was standard for ALL LSS with spondylolisthesis. SWESPINE overturned this dogma — adding fusion provided no benefit in ODI, walking capacity, or patient satisfaction at 2 years for STABLE spondylolisthesis. Routine fusion is no longer recommended by NASS 2020.
wrong.C: Spondylolisthesis is NOT a contraindication to surgery — it is a common comorbidity in LSS. Stable spondylolisthesis with classic neurogenic claudication symptoms is a surgical indication when conservative treatment has failed.
wrong.D: ALIF addresses disc height restoration and sagittal correction — it es apropiado for high-grade spondylolisthesis or significant sagittal imbalance, not for a 3 mm stable slip in an elderly patient. It would be significantly more invasive than the appropriate treatment.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — specifically addresses post-SWESPINE management: decompression alone is Grado B recommendation for stable spondylolisthesis without dynamic instability.
explanation.trial: Försth P et al. (SWESPINE). A Randomized, Controlled Ensayo of Fusion Surgery for Estenosis Espinal Lumbar. NEJM 2016;374:1413-1423. Primary outcome (ODI at 2 years): decompression -20.4 vs decompression+fusion -18.3 (P=0.24). No subgroup benefit for spondylolisthesis.
explanation.pearl: Post-SWESPINE (2016): for stable LSS ± spondylolisthesis — decompression alone es apropiado. Fusion is reserved for: dynamic instability (>4 mm translation on flex-ext films), progressive slip, coronal deformity, or iatrogenic destabilisation requiring reconstruction.

questions.stem: Which nerve root is compressed by hypertrophy of the SUPERIOR ARTICULAR PROCESS of L4 within the lateral recess at the L4-5 level?

options.A: L4 nerve root (exiting root)
options.B: L5 nerve root (traversing root)
options.C: S1 nerve root
options.D: L3 nerve root

explanation.correct: The lateral recess at the L4-5 level is the zone medial to the L5 pedicle, between the posterior vertebral body of L5 anteriorly and the superior articular process (SAP) of L4 posteriorly. The root traversing this zone — heading toward the L5-S1 foramen — is the L5 root. This is the 'traversing root' distinction: hypertrophy of the L4 SAP compresses the L5 traversing root before it reaches its own foramen. The L4 exiting root exits at the L4-5 foramen above this level.

wrong.A: The L4 exiting root exits via the L4-5 foramen, not via the lateral recess below. Foraminal stenosis at L4-5 affects the L4 exiting root. Lateral recess stenosis at L4-5 affects the L5 traversing root — an important distinction.
wrong.C: The S1 root traverses the L5-S1 lateral recess, not the L4-5 lateral recess. S1 root compression typically presents with plantar foot pain, Achilles reflex loss, and calf weakness.
wrong.D: The L3 root exits at the L3-4 foramen and traverses the L3-4 lateral recess. It is not involved at the L4-5 level.

explanation.guideline: AOSpine Degenerative Lumbar Stenosis Guidelines (2020) — anatomy review: lateral recess at L4-5 contains the L5 traversing root; foramen at L4-5 contains the L4 exiting root.
explanation.trial: Verbiest H. A radicular syndrome from developmental narrowing of the lumbar vertebral canal. JBNS 1954;7:435-450 — foundational anatomical description of lateral recess compression by SAP hypertrophy and its root-specific consequences.
explanation.pearl: Rule for root compression at any lumbar level: LATERAL RECESS stenosis compresses the TRAVERSING root (one number higher than the level — e.g., L4-5 lateral recess compresses L5). FORAMINAL stenosis compresses the EXITING root (same number as the level — e.g., L4-5 foramen compresses L4).

questions.stem: Una mujer de 58 años undergoes a BILATERAL DECOMPRESSION VIA UNILATERAL APPROACH (BDUA, 'over-the-top' technique) at L4-5 for central stenosis. The key advantage of this approach over standard bilateral total laminectomy is:

options.A: It allows complete removal of both L4 and L5 laminae with less bleeding
options.B: It preserves the supraspinous and interspinous ligaments and contralateral musculature, reducing iatrogenic instability
options.C: It eliminates the need for fluoroscopic level confirmation
options.D: It provides superior visualisation of the anterior espacio epidural for disc removal

explanation.correct: The BDUA (bilateral decompression via unilateral approach, 'over-the-top') technique performs a unilateral keyhole laminotomy and angles the operating microscope or endoscope across the midline to decompress the contralateral side. By preserving the spinous process, supraspinous ligament, interspinous ligament, and contralateral paraspinal musculature, it maintains posterior spinal stability (the posterior tension band). This significantly reduces the risk of post-operative spondylolisthesis progression or iatrogenic instability compared with total bilateral laminectomy, while achieving equivalent decompression of the bilateral lateral recesses.

wrong.A: BDUA does NOT remove both laminae — that is the definition of total laminectomy. BDUA removes one side's lamina partially (keyhole), then angles across to decompress the other side through the same window. Less bone is removed, not more.
wrong.C: Fluoroscopic level confirmation remains MANDATORY for all spinal surgery regardless of approach. Wrong-level surgery can occur with any technique and is a preventable, never-event complication.
wrong.D: BDUA is specifically designed for posterior element (ligamentum flavum, SAP) decompression. It does NOT provide superior anterior access. Anterior disc removal requires anterior approaches (ALIF) or posterior transforaminal approaches (TLIF).

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — evidence supports BDUA as equivalent to laminectomy for decompression with superior biomechanical preservation of posterior elements.
explanation.trial: Thome C et al. Resultado after less-invasive decompression of lumbar spinal stenosis: a randomized comparison of unilateral laminotomy, bilateral laminotomy, and laminectomy. J Neurosurg Spine 2005;3:129-141: unilateral laminotomy and BDUA = equivalent neurological outcomes to laminectomy, with lower instability rate and shorter hospital stay.
explanation.pearl: BDUA ('over-the-top') principle: one incision, one laminotomy, bilateral decompression. Key advantages: (1) preserves posterior tension band; (2) bilateral decompressions via single approach; (3) shorter hospital stay; (4) lower post-operative instability rate. Now preferred over total laminectomy for bilateral central LSS at most academic centres.

questions.stem: Una mujer obesa de 74 años (BMI 38) with lumbar spinal stenosis is referred for surgical evaluation. She has bilateral neurogenic claudication limiting her to 100 metres and significant impact on quality of life. She has been through 8 weeks of physiotherapy and two epidural steroid injections without benefit. MRI shows Schizas C stenosis at L3-4 and L4-5. Her comorbidities include: type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.2%), COPD (GOLD III), and mild chronic kidney disease (eGFR 48). What is the primary concern regarding surgical planning in this patient?

options.A: Obesity is an absolute contraindication to lumbar decompression surgery
options.B: COPD GOLD III precludes general anaesthesia — neuraxial anaesthesia debe usarse instead
options.C: Poor glycaemic control (HbA1c 9.2%) significantly increases infection risk and should be optimised before elective surgery
options.D: Chronic kidney disease eGFR 48 requires pre-operative haemodialysis

explanation.correct: HbA1c 9.2% indicates suboptimal glycaemic control and significantly increases the risk of post-operative wound infection, deep surgical site infection, and poor wound healing. Current guidelines recommend delaying elective spinal surgery until HbA1c is reduced to <8.0% (ideally <7.5%). Each 1% reduction in HbA1c approximately halves the surgical infection risk. This patient should be referred back to endocrinology/primary care for glucose optimisation before proceeding with elective decompression.

wrong.A: Obesity is a relative risk factor for increased wound complications and anaesthetic challenges but is NOT an absolute contraindication to lumbar decompression. Many obese patients undergo successful spinal surgery with appropriate risk stratification and technique modification.
wrong.B: COPD GOLD III increases pulmonary risk but does NOT absolutely preclude general anaesthesia. Spinal/neuraxial anaesthesia is a reasonable alternative for single-level decompression, but the COPD alone does not mandate this. Cardiopulmonary risk assessment (RCRI, spirometry) guides the anaesthetic choice.
wrong.D: eGFR 48 represents CKD Stage 3a/b — this is an important consideration for contrast avoidance (if using CT myelography) and NSAID restriction, but does NOT require pre-operative dialysis. Haemodialysis is typically initiated for eGFR <15 in symptomatic uraemia.

explanation.guideline: British Orthopaedic Association (BOA) Standards for Trauma and Elective Spinal Surgery (2020) — HbA1c ≥8.0% as threshold for delaying elective spinal surgery. NASS LSS Guidelines (2020) reference diabetic risk.
explanation.trial: Hikata T et al. Impact of HbA1c on perioperative complications in spine surgery. Spine 2014;39:1297-1302: HbA1c ≥7.5% independently associated with 4.3× increased surgical site infection risk; each 1% reduction associated with 40% decrease in infection odds.
explanation.pearl: Pre-operative HbA1c target before elective spinal surgery: <8.0% minimum, ideally <7.5%. This is the most modifiable risk factor for post-operative infection. BMI, CKD, and COPD are relative risk factors requiring assessment, but poorly controlled diabetes is the most critical modifiable concern in the peri-operative period.

questions.stem: Un paciente de 69 años reports progressive inability to walk more than 50 metres before bilateral leg heaviness forces him to stop. He was previously able to walk 2 km daily. He denies back pain. MRI shows marked lumbar stenosis at L2-3, L3-4, and L4-5. Sin embargo, the neurology registrar also notices on a quick cervical spine MRI ordered at the same time: cord compression at C5-6 with myelopathic signal change (T2 hyperintensity), mJOA score 12. ¿Cuál es el correcto surgical strategy?

options.A: Lumbar decompression first — the walking limitation is the dominant complaint
options.B: Cervical decompression first — cervical myelopathy takes priority due to risk of cord injury
options.C: Simultaneous cervical and lumbar surgery in a single anaesthetic
options.D: Treat both conservatively with cervical collar and lumbar physiotherapy

explanation.correct: Tandem cervical-lumbar stenosis requires careful sequencing. Cervical myelopathy with T2 signal change and mJOA 12 (moderate) represents risk of irreversible cord injury from even minor trauma. Furthermore, lumbar decompression can unmask or worsen cervical myelopathy by improving gait and thus increasing mechanical stress on the cervical cord through improved walking. The established surgical principle is: CERVICAL decompression first in tandem stenosis with significant myelopathy, followed by lumbar decompression if claudication symptoms persist after cervical surgery.

wrong.A: Treating the most symptomatic complaint first (lumbar claudication) is the intuitive but incorrect approach for tandem stenosis with significant cervical myelopathy. Decompressing the lumbar spine first can unmask cervical myelopathy by restoring ambulatory function, increasing cervical cord stress, and risk of acute deterioration.
wrong.C: Single-stage combined cervical-lumbar surgery is rarely indicated and carries excessive operative time, blood loss, and physiological stress, particularly in elderly patients. Some groups advocate staged same-admission procedures, but this is not standard practice for non-emergency presentations.
wrong.D: mJOA 12 represents moderate myelopathy with T2 signal change — conservative management is no longer appropriate. AOSpine guidelines recommend surgery for moderate-to-severe myelopathy. Cervical collar immobilisation is a temporising measure only.

explanation.guideline: AOSpine Cervical Spondylotic Myelopathy Guidelines (Fehlings et al. 2017): surgical decompression recommended for moderate (mJOA 12-14) and severe (mJOA <12) CSM; T2 signal change indicates established myelopathic injury — operate without further delay.
explanation.trial: Naderi S et al. Surgical outcome in tandem cervical and lumbar spinal stenosis. Acta Neurochir 1999;141:1225-1231: cervical surgery first followed by lumbar surgery resulted in best outcomes; lumbar-first strategy associated with cervical myelopathy deterioration in 23% of cases.
explanation.pearl: Tandem stenosis rule: when both cervical myelopathy and lumbar stenosis coexist, operate on the CERVICAL cord first. Cervical myelopathy poses irreversible cord injury risk and limits the patient's ability to walk — treating it first also improves the functional baseline for assessing residual lumbar symptoms.

questions.stem: A patient with L4-5 central stenosis and degenerative L4-5 spondylolisthesis undergoes decompressive laminectomy only (no fusion). Flexion-extension radiographs taken 12 months later show new L4-5 dynamic instability with 6 mm translation in flexion. He now has recurrent back pain and radicular leg symptoms. ¿Cuál es el más apropiado management?

options.A: Corsé lumbar durante 6 meses
options.B: Cirugía de revisión con fusión instrumentada L4-5 (TLIF + tornillos pediculares)
options.C: Infiltración epidural de esteroides en L4-5
options.D: Tracción lumbar combinada con fortalecimiento del core

explanation.correct: Post-laminectomy instability with progressive spondylolisthesis (new 6 mm dynamic translation on flexion-extension films) causing recurrent neurological symptoms is a clear indication for revision instrumented fusion. TLIF (transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion) with pedicle screw fixation restores biomechanical stability, achieves interbody fusion, and can decompress any recurrent root compression. This complication occurs in 5-15% after laminectomy and is more common in patients with pre-existing spondylolisthesis — supporting the argument for fusion at index surgery in selected patients despite SWESPINE data.

wrong.A: External bracing (lumbar corset) cannot stabilise a dynamic spinal instability with 6 mm translation — it provides comfort but no structural stability. Ongoing instability will cause continued neural injury and progressive deformity.
wrong.C: Epidural steroid injection addresses radicular pain temporarily but does not treat the underlying mechanical instability causing ongoing nerve root traction and compression. It is inappropriate as definitive management for progressive instability.
wrong.D: Core strengthening is an important preventive measure but cannot provide mechanical stability once significant dynamic instability (6 mm translation) is established. It may complement conservative management for minor instability but es insuficiente here.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — revision surgery with fusion is indicated for post-laminectomy instability with >4 mm dynamic translation and associated neurological symptoms.
explanation.trial: Fischgrund JS et al. Degenerative lumbar spondylolisthesis with spinal stenosis: a prospective, randomized study comparing decompressive laminectomy and arthrodesis with and without spinal instrumentation. Spine 1997;22:2807-2812: instrumented fusion superior to non-instrumented for fusion rate (82% vs 45%) in spondylolisthesis — context for understanding instability risk.
explanation.pearl: Post-laminectomy instability threshold requiring fusion: >4 mm dynamic translation on flexion-extension films + clinical symptoms. Pedicle screw-based TLIF provides immediate rigid fixation and interbody fusion for durable stabilisation. This is the preventable complication that motivates selective primary fusion for high-risk spondylolisthesis.

questions.stem: Una mujer de 55 años presents with right L4 radiculopathy (quadriceps weakness MRC 4/5, positive femoral nerve stretch test, reduced knee jerk). MRI shows right-sided foraminal stenosis at L3-4 due to a large facet osteophyte compressing the L4 exiting root. She has failed 8 weeks of conservative management. The BEST surgical option is:

options.A: L3-4 bilateral laminectomy with foraminotomía bilaterally
options.B: L3-4 TLIF with cage and pedicle screws to increase foraminal height
options.C: Right-sided unilateral foraminotomía via keyhole laminotomy
options.D: Far-lateral Wiltse paraespinal approach to the L3-4 foramen

explanation.correct: Isolated unilateral foraminal stenosis compressing the exiting L4 root at L3-4 is optimally treated with a unilateral keyhole laminotomy and foraminotomy — targeted undercutting of the medial superior articular process of L4 and resection of the osteophyte compressing the root. This approach accesses the foramen from a posterior interlaminar window. The far-lateral Wiltse approach is reserved for EXTRAFORAMINAL (far-lateral) disc herniations or osteophytes lateral to the foramen, which are beyond the reach of standard interlaminar approaches.

wrong.A: Bilateral laminectomy with bilateral foraminotomía es excesivo for a unilateral foraminal problem. It removes more bone than needed, increases instability risk, and extends operative time without additional benefit.
wrong.B: TLIF with indirect decompression (cage distraction restoring foraminal height) is a valid strategy for foraminal stenosis due to disc height loss, particularly when fusion is also indicated. Sin embargo, for an isolated facet osteophyte compressing the foramen without instability, direct foraminotomía is simpler, less invasive, and equally effective.
wrong.D: The Wiltse paraespinal approach is specifically indicated for EXTRAFORAMINAL (far-lateral) pathology — lateral to the lateral border of the foramen. Foraminal stenosis (within the foramen) is accessible via the standard posterior interlaminar keyhole approach. Using Wiltse for intraforaminal pathology would not allow adequate visualisation.

explanation.guideline: AOSpine Degenerative Lumbar Stenosis Guidelines (2020) — foraminal stenosis causing unilateral radiculopathy without instability: targeted unilateral foraminotomía is the procedure of choice; fusion not routinely indicated.
explanation.trial: Katz JN et al. Predictors of surgical outcome in patients with lumbosacro disc disease. Spine 1999;24:2529-2535: unilateral radiculopathy with clear imaging-clinical correlation predicts excellent surgical outcome (>85% good/excellent at 4 years) with minimal decompression.
explanation.pearl: Foraminal (intraforaminal) stenosis → posterior keyhole laminotomy + foraminotomía. Far-lateral (extraforaminal) stenosis → Wiltse paraespinal approach. The key anatomical distinction is the position of pathology relative to the foramen's lateral border. Getting this wrong leads to failed decompressions.

questions.stem: The Verbiest classification of lumbar spinal stenosis defines 'absolute stenosis' as an anteroposterior canal diameter of:

options.A: Less than 15 mm
options.B: Less than 12 mm
options.C: Less than 10 mm
options.D: Less than 8 mm

explanation.correct: Verbiest (1954) defined absolute stenosis as AP canal diameter <10 mm and relative stenosis as 10-12 mm. Normal AP lumbar canal diameter is 15-25 mm. While cross-sectional area measurements (<75 mm² relative, with significant clinical stenosis typically <100 mm²) and the Schizas grading system are now preferred in contemporary practice, the Verbiest AP diameter criteria remain historically important and frequently tested in examinations.

wrong.A: <15 mm is the lower limit of the normal range — this is not Verbiest's threshold for stenosis. Values between 12-15 mm may represent mild canal narrowing without stenosis.
wrong.B: <12 mm is Verbiest's 'relative stenosis' threshold (10-12 mm range). Relative stenosis may be symptomatic with concurrent soft tissue compression; absolute stenosis is the more severe category defined as <10 mm.
wrong.D: <8 mm would represent extreme narrowing, more consistent with severe congenital stenosis (achondroplasia) or severe absolute stenosis beyond Verbiest's threshold. It is not the defined threshold for absolute stenosis.

explanation.guideline: Verbiest H (1954) — foundational publication establishing lumbar stenosis criteria: absolute <10 mm, relative 10-12 mm. Referenced in NASS Guidelines (2020) historical context and AOSpine anatomy review.
explanation.trial: Verbiest H. A radicular syndrome from developmental narrowing of the lumbar vertebral canal. J Bone Joint Surg Br 1954;36:230-237 — seminal paper describing lumbar spinal stenosis as a clinical-anatomical entity and establishing diameter thresholds still used today.
explanation.pearl: Verbiest criteria: absolute stenosis AP diameter <10 mm; relative stenosis 10-12 mm; normal 15-25 mm. Modern practice favours cross-sectional area (<75 mm² relative, <100 mm² borderline) and Schizas grading (A-D on axial T2), but Verbiest thresholds are board-exam staples.

questions.stem: Una mujer de 63 años is evaluated for lumbar spinal stenosis surgery. ¿Cuál de los siguientes Waddell signs, when present, is most predictive of a POOR surgical outcome?

options.A: Positive straight leg raise test at 60 degrees
options.B: Tenderness to superficial palpation over a wide area of the lumbar spine
options.C: Positive crossed straight leg raise test
options.D: Reduced lumbar range of motion on forward flexion

explanation.correct: Waddell signs are non-organic physical signs indicating that psychological or behavioural factors contribute significantly to the patient's symptom experience. Superficial tenderness to light touch over a wide and non-anatomical area of the lumbar spine is a classic Waddell sign. When three or more Waddell signs are present, the patient is considered a poor surgical candidate with high risk of persistent symptoms despite technically successful decompression. Other Waddell signs include: regional weakness or sensory change that does not follow dermatomal or myotomal patterns, pain with simulated spinal loading (axial loading while patient stands), and improvement in the straight leg raise when the patient is distracted.

wrong.A: A positive SLR at 60 degrees is a ORGANIC sign of lumbar nerve root tension — it is not a Waddell sign. A positive SLR (radicular pain reproduced below the knee) suggests genuine disc herniation or root compression and is a favourable predictor of surgical outcome when correlated with MRI.
wrong.C: A positive crossed SLR (contralateral leg pain when the ipsilateral leg is raised) has high specificity (88%) for genuine nerve root compression and is an organic physical sign — not a Waddell sign.
wrong.D: Reduced lumbar range of motion on forward flexion is a common organic sign of lumbar pathology (degeneration, stenosis, disc disease) and is not a Waddell sign. It does not predict poor surgical outcome.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — patient selection: psychological risk factors and Waddell signs assessed as part of pre-surgical evaluation; ≥3 Waddell signs = poor surgical prognosis.
explanation.trial: Waddell G et al. Nonorganic physical signs in low-back pain. Spine 1980;5:117-125 — original description of 5 categories of non-organic signs; presence of ≥3 signs predictive of poor surgical outcome and high disability.
explanation.pearl: Waddell signs (≥3 = poor prognosis): (1) tenderness — superficial/non-anatomical; (2) simulation — axial loading or rotation produces pain; (3) distraction — SLR positive when formal but negative when distracted; (4) regional — non-dermatomal weakness/sensory; (5) overreaction — exaggerated pain behaviour. These are not malingering indicators but reflect central sensitisation and psychosocial impact.

questions.stem: Un hombre de 67 años with severe L3-4 central stenosis and neurogenic claudication at 80 metres undergoes L3-4 bilateral decompression. At 3-month follow-up, he reports excellent relief of claudication (can walk 1 km) but persistent moderate lower back pain (VAS 5/10). He had significant pre-operative axial back pain alongside his claudication. ¿Cuál de los siguientes best explains this residual symptom?

options.A: Inadequate decompression — the surgery has failed
options.B: Post-operative epidural fibrosis causing recurrent root compression
options.C: Axial back pain has a different aetiology (facetogenic or discogenic) not addressed by decompression
options.D: Iatrogenic instability requiring revision fusion

explanation.correct: This patient's claudication (the surgical target) has resolved completely (walking 1 km vs 80 m pre-op) — confirming successful decompression. The persistent axial back pain reflects a separate pain generator: facetogenic pain from arthritic facet joints or discogenic pain from degenerated disc, neither of which is addressed by laminectomy/flavectomy. Axial back pain is a relative contraindication to decompression alone (NASS guidelines) because it is unlikely to be relieved by decompression of the neural elements. Pre-operative patient counselling should always address this distinction — decompression treats claudication, not axial back pain.

wrong.A: The claudication outcome is excellent — the decompression has succeeded for its intended purpose. Persistent axial back pain in the context of resolved claudication does not indicate surgical failure.
wrong.B: Epidural fibrosis typically presents with recurrent radicular symptoms (leg pain, new neurological signs) within 3-12 months post-op, not isolated axial back pain in the absence of new neurological findings.
wrong.D: Iatrogenic instability presents with mechanical back pain that is worse with loading/movement, often associated with recurrent leg symptoms and demonstrated on flexion-extension films. Three months after surgery is an appropriate check point, but the clinical picture (resolved claudication, persistent pre-existing back pain) points to facetogenic/discogenic aetiology rather than instability.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — pre-operative counselling: patients should be informed that decompression is effective for neurogenic claudication but NOT for axial back pain; pre-operative axial back pain is an independent predictor of residual back pain post-operatively.
explanation.trial: Weinstein JN et al. SPORT Spinal Stenosis (NEJM 2008): as-treated surgical group showed greater improvement in leg pain (claudication) than back pain at all time points — confirming that decompression differentially addresses claudication over axial back pain.
explanation.pearl: Decompression treats CLAUDICATION (neural compression), not AXIAL BACK PAIN (facetogenic/discogenic). The pre-operative pain pattern predicts the post-operative residual: patients with predominantly axial back pain pre-op will still have back pain post-op even with excellent claudication relief. Patient counselling must address this clearly.

questions.stem: ¿Cuál de los siguientes imaging findings on STANDING lateral lumbar radiograph most strongly indicates the need for fusion in addition to decompression for lumbar spinal stenosis?

options.A: Disc space height reduction of 50% at L4-5 compared with L3-4
options.B: Facet joint arthrosis grade 3 with vacuum phenomenon
options.C: Anterior translation of L4 on L5 of 8 mm on flexion film, not present on extension film
options.D: Osteophyte formation at the L4 and L5 endplates (traction osteophytes)

explanation.correct: Dynamic instability — defined as >4 mm translation of one vertebral body on the adjacent one between flexion and extension lateral radiographs — is the most evidence-based radiological indication for adding fusion to decompression. An 8 mm dynamic translation (present in flexion, absent in extension) at L4-5 clearly exceeds this threshold and represents significant segmental instability that will likely worsen after laminectomy removes the posterior stabilising elements. Fusion is necessary to prevent progressive neurological compromise.

wrong.A: Significant disc height loss (>50%) reflects advanced degeneration and may reduce foraminal height, but alone — without dynamic instability — it is not an indication for fusion. Many patients with severe disc degeneration have STABLE segments and can be treated with decompression alone.
wrong.B: Vacuum phenomenon and grade 3 facet arthrosis indicate severe degeneration but not dynamic instability per se. Advanced degeneration may paradoxically be associated with autostabilisation (osteophyte bridging) — these patients sometimes have LESS dynamic instability despite more radiological degeneration.
wrong.D: Traction osteophytes at the endplate margins (horizontal osteophytes 2-3 mm above the disc space) are associated with segmental hypermobility and degeneration, but their presence alone does not constitute an indication for fusion in the absence of documented dynamic translation.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — fusion indication: dynamic instability >4 mm translation on flexion-extension radiographs is a Grado B recommendation for adding instrumented fusion to decompression.
explanation.trial: Herkowitz HN, Kurz LT. Degenerative lumbar spondylolisthesis with spinal stenosis: prospective study comparing decompression with decompression and intertransverse process arthrodesis. J Bone Joint Surg Am 1991;73:802-808: laminectomy + arthrodesis superior to laminectomy alone for symptomatic spondylolisthesis at 2-3 years follow-up — historical basis for fusion threshold.
explanation.pearl: Dynamic instability threshold for fusion: >4 mm translation on flexion-extension lateral radiographs. This radiological finding overrides SWESPINE data (which studied STABLE spondylolisthesis) — patients with documented dynamic instability should receive decompression + fusion, not decompression alone.

questions.stem: Un hombre de 70 años with multilevel lumbar spinal stenosis (L2-3, L3-4, L4-5) is planned for decompressive surgery. During the operation, the surgeon is uncertain which level is being operated on. What is the gold-standard method for confirming operative level?

options.A: Counting spinous processes from C7 downward by palpation
options.B: Intraoperative fluoroscopic (C-arm) lateral radiograph with a marker placed in the wound
options.C: Reviewing pre-operative MRI at the operative table from memory
options.D: Needle localisation under CT guidance performed the morning before surgery

explanation.correct: Intraoperative fluoroscopic level confirmation using a metallic marker (Kocher clamp, Steinmann pin, or needle) placed at the operated level, with a lateral C-arm radiograph, is the gold standard for confirming operative spinal level. This technique provides real-time anatomical confirmation referenced to a visible skeletal landmark (sacrum, ilium, or L5-S1 disc). Wrong-level surgery is a never-event in spinal surgery — it is preventable and its occurrence constitutes a serious adverse event. Fluoroscopic level confirmation should be performed BEFORE making the critical operative commitment at each case.

wrong.A: Counting spinous processes by palpation is unreliable in obese patients, elderly patients with osteoporosis, those with previous spinal surgery, and cases with anatomical variants (sacroization, lumbarisation). Palpation alone without imaging confirmation is not acceptable as sole level verification in spinal surgery.
wrong.C: Relying on memory of pre-operative MRI images to navigate intraoperatively is not an acceptable level confirmation technique. The surgeon's visual field after soft tissue dissection does not reliably correspond to memory of imaging without real-time radiological confirmation.
wrong.D: Pre-operative CT-guided needle localisation is occasionally used in complex revision cases but is not the intraoperative gold standard and cannot substitute for intraoperative confirmation — needle position may change from pre-op positioning to intraoperative prone positioning.

explanation.guideline: WHO Safe Surgery Checklist — spinal surgery team time-out includes level confirmation. NASS and BOA standards: intraoperative fluoroscopic level confirmation is mandatory before any spinal decompression. Wrong-level surgery is a reportable never-event.
explanation.trial: Mody MG et al. The prevalence of wrong level surgery among spine surgeons. Spine 2008;33:194-198: wrong-level surgery estimated at 1:3,110 cases; 78% of surgeons reported at least one wrong-level case in their career — confirming systematic fluoroscopic confirmation as critical preventive measure.
explanation.pearl: Never operate on a spinal level without intraoperative fluoroscopic confirmation with a metallic marker in the wound. Counting spinous processes or using pre-operative imaging memory alone es insuficiente. Wrong-level surgery is a preventable never-event.

questions.stem: Una mujer de 76 años with severe lumbar spinal stenosis (Schizas C at L3-4, L4-5) and neurogenic claudication at 30 metres undergoes successful two-level bilateral decompression via unilateral approach. Five years post-operatively, she returns with recurrence of bilateral claudication at 150 metres. MRI shows Schizas B recurrent stenosis at both previously decompressed levels due to new ligamentum flavum re-hypertrophy and facet arthrosis. There is no instability on dynamic films. ¿Cuál es el más apropiado management?

options.A: Immediate revision laminectomy with instrumented fusion at both levels
options.B: Conservative management with physiotherapy and epidural steroid injections, with revision decompression considered if symptoms progress
options.C: Intrathecal opioid pump for long-term neurogenic pain management
options.D: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) implantation at T10-T12

explanation.correct: Recurrent stenosis 5 years after successful decompression, with moderate severity (Schizas B, reduced from Schizas C at original surgery), and no instability, should initially be managed with structured conservative treatment. Epidural steroid injection may provide 3-6 months of symptom relief. Revision surgery (revision decompression) is technically feasible and effective but carries higher complication risk than primary surgery (particularly dural tear rate 14% vs 5% in revision). Given the 5-year disease-free interval and moderate current severity, conservative measures should be tried first, with revision decompression reserved for failure or significant deterioration.

wrong.A: Immediate revision laminectomy PLUS instrumented fusion es excesivo — fusion no está indicado without instability. Revision decompression alone (if surgery is ultimately needed) is the appropriate procedure. Adding fusion in an elderly patient without instability increases operative risk substantially.
wrong.C: Intrathecal opioid pump is indicated for chronic non-cancer pain syndromes that have failed all other treatments, including failed back surgery syndrome. It no es apropiado as second-line management after a single episode of recurrent stenosis with a 5-year disease-free interval.
wrong.D: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) is an evidence-based treatment for failed back surgery syndrome (FBSS) and neurogenic claudication refractory to medical/surgical management. Sin embargo, this patient has not yet failed conservative treatment for the current recurrence and has not undergone revision surgery — SCS would be premature at this stage.

explanation.guideline: NASS Clinical Practice Guidelines for LSS (2020) — recurrent stenosis: conservative management is the appropriate initial approach for patients with moderate recurrence and adequate prior disease-free interval; revision surgery indicated for severe recurrence or failure of conservative measures.
explanation.trial: Katz JN et al. 10-year results of non-operative care in patients with lumbar spinal stenosis. Spine 2005;30:2314-2319: 10-year outcomes in non-operative LSS showed gradual progression in the majority but with substantial heterogeneity — supports individualised rather than automatic surgical management for recurrence.
explanation.pearl: Recurrent stenosis after successful decompression: (1) conservative measures first (physio, ESI, activity modification); (2) revision decompression if conservative fails (dural tear risk 14% in revision — inform patient); (3) add fusion ONLY if instability develops. Spinal cord stimulation is reserved for FBSS refractory to all other measures.
